title: Why Do External Rewards Eventually Kill Internal Drive?

situation.content:
You loved doing it — the side project, the creative writing, the volunteer work. Then someone offered to pay you for it. Or your company gave you a bonus for something you were already doing well. And slowly, without anyone noticing, the thing that used to pull you forward started feeling like an obligation.

The money should have made it better. Instead it made it... work.

story.content:
Viktor Korchnoi started playing chess at age six in Leningrad. Not because anyone told him to — because the patterns on the board seized him. He played through the siege of Leningrad as a child, through starvation and bombardment, because the sixty-four squares were the one place where the world made sense and he had agency.

By the 1960s, he was one of the strongest players in the Soviet Union. And the Soviet Union had decided that chess was a weapon. Korchnoi was given a state apartment, a stipend, access to training facilities, coaching staff, seconds, and a KGB minder. His tournament results determined whether he kept the apartment. His world ranking determined whether his son could attend university. Every game now carried the weight of his family's material life.

He kept winning. But something had changed. In interviews from that era, he described feeling a 'heaviness' that hadn't existed before. The board that once was freedom had become a ledger. Each game was no longer exploration — it was debt payment. He played not to discover but to not lose what he'd been given.

In 1976, during a tournament in Amsterdam, Korchnoi made his decision. He walked into the Dutch police station and requested political asylum. He left behind his wife, his son, his apartment, his stipend, his coaching team, his country. Everything the Soviet system had given him in exchange for his chess.

At the press conference, a journalist asked why. His answer: 'I used to love this game. Somewhere it became my job, then my obligation, then my prison. I am forty-five years old. I would like to love it again before I die.'

He did. In exile, with no state support, no seconds, no guaranteed income, playing for whatever prize money Western tournaments offered, Korchnoi played some of the most creative, aggressive chess of his career. He challenged for the World Championship in 1978 at age forty-seven — an age when most players have long retired. He continued competing at the highest level into his seventies.

The Soviet system hadn't made him better. It had made him reliable. And reliability is what remains when love leaves.

contrast.content: Mark Twain captured this in one line: 'Work consists of whatever a body is obliged to do. Play consists of whatever a body is not obliged to do.' He observed that wealthy English gentlemen drove four-horse coaches twenty miles in summer for fun — but if offered wages for it, they'd resign. The introduction of obligation, even profitable obligation, transforms the nature of the activity without changing a single physical detail. Korchnoi's chess didn't change. His relationship to it did.

principle.content:
External rewards don't add to internal motivation — they replace it. And replacements, once established, can't be removed without leaving a vacuum.

The most dangerous incentive isn't too little reward. It's a reward that teaches you to forget why you cared in the first place.

psychology.content:
This is the overjustification effect (Lepper, Greene & Nisbett, 1973), one of the most replicated findings in motivational psychology. In the original study, children who loved drawing were divided into three groups: one was promised a reward for drawing, one received a surprise reward, and one got nothing. Two weeks later, the children who'd been promised the reward drew significantly less during free time. The promise had converted play into transaction.

The mechanism is cognitive reappraisal: the brain asks 'why am I doing this?' and if an external answer is available — money, status, housing — it overwrites the internal answer of curiosity, mastery, or joy. Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan, 1985) identifies three intrinsic drivers: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Contingent external rewards undermine all three by shifting the locus of control from self to system.

Korchnoi's story shows the full arc: intrinsic drive → external reward attached → drive replaced by obligation → removal of reward leaves vacuum → deliberate return to zero to rediscover the original drive. The defection wasn't political. It was motivational surgery — cutting away everything that had grown over the original love.

move.content:
1. Audit your motivational fuel. For your most important work, ask: am I still doing this because I want to, or because I'm being rewarded for it? If the reward disappeared tomorrow, would you continue? If not, the reward has already replaced your drive.

2. Protect the playground. Keep at least one creative activity completely unmonetized and unevaluated. The moment everything you love has a price tag, you have nothing left that's intrinsically yours. Korchnoi had to defect to find his playground again. You can protect yours cheaper.

3. Notice when obligation replaces curiosity. The signal: you start optimizing for the metric instead of the craft. When 'how do I make this great?' becomes 'how do I hit the target?' — that's the replacement happening in real time.

4. Use surprise recognition, never contingent promises. When recognizing others' work, make it unexpected. 'If you do X, you'll get Y' converts play to labor. 'I noticed what you did — here's something for it' reinforces without replacing. The difference is whether the reward existed as a reason before the work began.

5. Schedule the return to zero. Once a year, spend a week doing your craft with no audience, no deadline, no metric, no client. Just you and the thing itself. If it still pulls you — good, the love is alive. If it doesn't — that's information about what the rewards have done to your relationship with the work.
